title: Search for a value in a binary tree
description: Check if a given value exists in the binary tree (return true/false)

examples.explanation:
1. Start at root node 1: value 1 ≠ 5 → search left subtree
2. Move to node 2: value 2 ≠ 5 → search left subtree
3. Move to node 4: value 4 ≠ 5 → left is null → right is null → return false
4. Back to node 2: left returned false → search right subtree
5. Move to node 5: value 5 = 5 → return true
6. Back to node 2: right returned true → return true
7. Back to node 1: left returned true → return true (found)

1. Start at root node 1: value 1 ≠ 4 → search left subtree
2. Move to node 2: value 2 ≠ 4 → left is null → right is null → return false
3. Back to node 1: left returned false → search right subtree
4. Move to node 3: value 3 ≠ 4 → left is null → right is null → return false
5. Back to node 1: right returned false → return false (not found)

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(n)
approaches.summary: Recursively traverse the entire binary tree using depth-first search (DFS) to check each node's value against the target. This is straightforward and works for any binary tree structure.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the tree height (due to recursion stack)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search for a value in a binary tree using brute force DFS
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution to check if a target value exists in any node of a binary tree
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force DFS approach to search for a value in a binary tree. The intuition is simple: we need to examine every node in the tree to ensure we don't miss the target value. Since binary trees don't have any ordering guarantees (unlike binary search trees), we must traverse the entire structure. The algorithm works by recursively visiting each node, checking if its value matches the target, and if not, continuing the search in both left and right subtrees. The base case occurs when we reach a null node (indicating we've reached the end of a branch without finding the target). The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we may need to visit every node in the worst case. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height, due to the recursion stack depth. This approach connects directly to L1 by implementing the complete recursive search function. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle null nodes properly (leading to null pointer errors), not returning the OR of both subtree results (which would stop after finding the target in one branch), and misunderstanding the difference between this approach and binary search tree search. The real pattern involves a clean recursive function that checks the current node, then recursively searches both subtrees.

examples.trace:
1. Start at root node 1: value 1 ≠ 5 → search left subtree
2. Move to node 2: value 2 ≠ 5 → search left subtree
3. Move to node 4: value 4 ≠ 5 → left is null → right is null → return false
4. Back to node 2: left returned false → search right subtree
5. Move to node 5: value 5 = 5 → return true
6. Back to node 2: right returned true → return true
7. Back to node 1: left returned true → return true (found)

1. Start at root node 1: value 1 ≠ 4 → search left subtree
2. Move to node 2: value 2 ≠ 4 → left is null → right is null → return false
3. Back to node 1: left returned false → search right subtree
4. Move to node 3: value 3 ≠ 4 → left is null → right is null → return false
5. Back to node 1: right returned false → return false (not found)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS traversal to search a binary tree
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that traverses the tree and checks for target value
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive function that performs the DFS search. The intuition is that searching a tree naturally lends itself to recursion because each subtree is itself a tree. The algorithm takes a node and target value as input. If the node is null, we return false (base case). Otherwise, we check if the current node's value matches the target. If it does, we return true immediately. If not, we recursively search the left subtree. If the left search returns true, we can return true immediately (short-circuiting). Otherwise, we search the right subtree and return whatever it finds. The time complexity for this function is O(n) in the worst case where we traverse all nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the complete implementation, and to L2 as it builds upon the core logic of checking nodes and traversing subtrees. Common mistakes include not using short-circuit evaluation (searching right subtree even when left found the target), forgetting the null check, and not understanding that we need to search both subtrees. The pattern is a clean recursive function with proper base case and recursive case.

examples.trace:
1. Node 5: value 5 ≠ 7 → search left subtree
2. Node 3: value 3 ≠ 7 → left is null → right is null → return false
3. Back to node 5: left returned false → search right subtree
4. Node 7: value 7 = 7 → return true
5. Back to node 5: right returned true → return true

1. Node 10: value 10 ≠ 20 → search left subtree
2. Node 5: value 5 ≠ 20 → left is null → right is null → return false
3. Back to node 10: left returned false → search right subtree
4. Node 15: value 15 ≠ 20 → left is null → right is null → return false
5. Back to node 10: right returned false → return false

ladders.title: Core Logic — Check current node and traverse both subtrees
ladders.desc: Understand the fundamental pattern of checking a node and recursively searching its children
ladders.explanation: This level captures the essential logic pattern: at each node, we perform two operations—check if the current node contains the target value, and if not, continue searching in both subtrees. The intuition is that each node in a binary tree has up to two children (left and right), and the target could be in any of these three positions (current node, left subtree, or right subtree). We must check all three possibilities. The algorithm pseudocode is: if node is null → return false; if node.value equals target → return true; otherwise → return search(left) OR search(right). The OR operator is crucial because it implements short-circuit evaluation—if the target is found in the left subtree, we don't need to search the right subtree. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node, but when applied recursively to all nodes it becomes O(n). Space complexity consideration starts here with the recursion stack. This connects to L1 as the pattern used in the recursive function, and to L3 which provides the tree traversal mechanism. Common mistakes include checking only one subtree, not using OR properly, and misunderstanding when to return true vs false. The pattern is a decision structure that handles all possibilities at a node.

examples.input: Current node value: 10, Target: 10, Left result: (don't care), Right result: (don't care)
examples.trace:
1. Check if node is null: no
2. Check if node.value (10) equals target (10): yes
3. Return true immediately (don't check subtrees)

examples.input: Current node value: 10, Target: 5, Left result: false, Right result: true
examples.trace:
1. Check if node is null: no
2. Check if node.value (10) equals target (5): no
3. Check left subtree result: false
4. Check right subtree result: true
5. Return left OR right = false OR true = true

testCases.input: Node value: 5, Target: 5, Left: false, Right: false
Node value: 5, Target: 3, Left: true, Right: false
Node value: 5, Target: 3, Left: false, Right: true
Node value: 5, Target: 3, Left: false, Right: false
Node value: 5, Target: 3, Left: true, Right: true
Node is null, Target: 5, Left: N/A, Right: N/A
Node value: 10, Target: 10, Left: true, Right: false
Node value: 10, Target: 10, Left: false, Right: true
Node value: 10, Target: 15, Left: false, Right: false
Node value: 10, Target: 15, Left: true, Right: false

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse a binary tree using recursion
ladders.desc: Learn how to recursively visit all nodes in a binary tree
ladders.explanation: This level teaches the fundamental skill of traversing a binary tree using recursion, which is essential for the search algorithm. The intuition is that a binary tree is a recursive data structure—each node has left and right subtrees that are themselves binary trees (or null). To visit all nodes, we can use a simple recursive approach: visit the current node, then recursively traverse the left subtree, then recursively traverse the right subtree (this is pre-order traversal, though the order doesn't matter for search). The algorithm: if node is null → return (base case); otherwise → process current node; traverse(left); traverse(right). Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack. This connects to L2 as the traversal mechanism that enables checking all nodes, and to L4 which provides the basic node access operations. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (forgetting base case), stack overflow for deep trees, and confusion about different traversal orders. The pattern is a simple recursive function that handles the null case and calls itself on child nodes.

examples.trace:
1. Start at root node 1: visit 1
2. Traverse left subtree (node 2): visit 2
3. Node 2 has no left child → return
4. Node 2 has no right child → return
5. Back to node 1: traverse right subtree (node 3): visit 3
6. Node 3 has no left child → return
7. Node 3 has no right child → return
8. Done

1. Start at root node 1: visit 1
2. Left subtree is null → return
3. Traverse right subtree (node 2): visit 2
4. Node 2 has no left child → return
5. Traverse right subtree (node 3): visit 3
6. Node 3 has no left child → return
7. Node 3 has no right child → return
8. Done

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access node value and child pointers
ladders.desc: Learn how to work with binary tree node structure: get value, check left/right children
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations needed to work with binary tree nodes. In most programming languages, a binary tree node is represented as an object or structure with three fields: value (the data stored), left (pointer to left child), and right (pointer to right child). The intuition is that these three pieces of information are all we need to traverse and search the tree. The basic operations are: 1) Access the node's value (node.value), 2) Check if left child exists (node.left != null), 3) Check if right child exists (node.right != null), 4) Access left child (node.left), 5) Access right child (node.right). Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the operations themselves. This connects to L3 as these operations enable tree traversal, and to L5 which provides the concept of what a node is. Common mistakes include trying to access value on null pointer, not checking for null before accessing children, and confusion between node value and node reference. The pattern is simple field access with null checks.

examples.output: Value: 10, Has left: true, Has right: true, Left value: 5, Right value: 15
examples.trace:
1. Access node.value → 10
2. Check node.left != null → true (points to node with value 5)
3. Check node.right != null → true (points to node with value 15)
4. Access node.left.value → 5
5. Access node.right.value → 15

examples.output: Value: 7, Has left: false, Has right: false
examples.trace:
1. Access node.value → 7
2. Check node.left != null → false
3. Check node.right != null → false
4. Cannot access node.left.value (would be null pointer error)
5. Cannot access node.right.value (would be null pointer error)

testCases.expected: Value: 5, Has left: false, Has right: false
Value: 8, Has left: true, Has right: false, Left value: 3
Value: 12, Has left: false, Has right: true, Right value: 20
Value: 15, Has left: true, Has right: true, Left value: 10, Right value: 25
Value: 100, Has left: true, Has right: true, Left value: 50, Right value: 150
Value: 1, Has left: true, Has right: true, Left value: 2, Right value: 3
Value: 9, Has left: false, Has right: true, Right value: 18
Value: 6, Has left: true, Has right: false, Left value: 4
Value: 30, Has left: true, Has right: true, Left value: 25, Right value: 35
Value: 0, Has left: false, Has right: false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a binary tree node?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree node
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces what a binary tree node is—the fundamental building block of binary trees. A binary tree node is a simple data structure that contains three pieces of information: 1) A value (can be any data type—integer, string, etc.), 2) A reference/pointer to a left child node (or null if no left child), 3) A reference/pointer to a right child node (or null if no right child). The intuition is that each node is like a container that holds data and connections to at most two other containers. This creates a hierarchical structure where nodes are organized in levels. In memory, nodes are typically allocated separately and connected via pointers/references. The concept of null is crucial—it represents the absence of a child. Time and space complexity aren't applicable at this basic conceptual level. This connects to L4 which uses this structure for operations. Common mistakes include thinking nodes store more than one value, confusing node references with node values, and not understanding that null is a valid child pointer. The pattern is a simple struct/class with three fields.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10, no children
examples.trace:
1. Allocate memory for a new node
2. Set node.value = 10
3. Set node.left = null (no left child)
4. Set node.right = null (no right child)
5. Result is a leaf node with value 10

examples.input: Create a node with value 5, left child with value 3, right child with value 7
examples.trace:
1. Create left child node: value=3, left=null, right=null
2. Create right child node: value=7, left=null, right=null
3. Create parent node: value=5
4. Set parent.left = reference to left child node
5. Set parent.right = reference to right child node
6. Result is a node with value 5 connected to two children

testCases.input: Node with value 2, left child value 1, no right child
Node with value 3, no left child, right child value 4
Node with value 5, left child value 4, right child value 6
Node with value 10, left child value 5, right child value 15
Node with value 8, left child value 7, right child value 9
Node with value 12, left child value 10, no right child
Node with value 20, no left child, right child value 25
Node with value 100, left child value 50, right child value 150

approaches.name: Iterative BFS O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a queue to perform breadth-first search (BFS), checking nodes level by level until the target is found or all nodes are visited. This approach explores the tree in a different order than DFS.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (due to queue storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative BFS Search in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of BFS to check if a value exists in a binary tree
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using iterative BFS to search for a value in a binary tree. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, which can be useful when you want to find the shallowest occurrence of a value or when dealing with trees where depth information matters. We use a queue data structure to maintain the order of node exploration. Starting from the root, we enqueue it, then repeatedly dequeue a node, check if it contains the target value, and if not, enqueue its children (if they exist). This continues until we either find the target or exhaust all nodes. The algorithm works because BFS systematically visits every node exactly once, guaranteeing we'll find the target if it exists. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we might visit all nodes in worst case. Space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree, which occurs when we store an entire level in the queue. This connects to L1 by using the queue management as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for null root, not handling null children properly when enqueuing, and infinite loops from improper queue management. Real code pattern: initialize queue with root, while queue not empty: dequeue node, check value, if match return true, enqueue left then right children if they exist, return false after loop.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize queue = [1]
2. Dequeue 1: value=1 ≠ 5, enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]
3. Dequeue 2: value=2 ≠ 5, enqueue children 4 and 5 → queue = [3,4,5]
4. Dequeue 3: value=3 ≠ 5, enqueue child 7 → queue = [4,5,7]
5. Dequeue 4: value=4 ≠ 5, no children → queue = [5,7]
6. Dequeue 5: value=5 = 5 → return true

1. Initialize queue = [10]
2. Dequeue 10: value=10 ≠ 8, enqueue 5 and 15 → queue = [5,15]
3. Dequeue 5: value=5 ≠ 8, enqueue 2 and 7 → queue = [15,2,7]
4. Dequeue 15: value=15 ≠ 8, enqueue 20 → queue = [2,7,20]
5. Dequeue 2: value=2 ≠ 8, no children → queue = [7,20]
6. Dequeue 7: value=7 ≠ 8, no children → queue = [20]
7. Dequeue 20: value=20 ≠ 8, no children → queue empty → return false

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Queue Management for BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the core queue operations for BFS traversal without the search logic
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the queue management aspect of BFS traversal. The intuition is that a queue (First-In-First-Out) naturally implements level-order traversal because we process nodes in the order they're discovered. We start by enqueuing the root, then enter a loop: dequeue a node, process it (just print/store for now), then enqueue its left and right children if they exist. This ensures we visit all nodes level by level. The algorithm: initialize empty queue, enqueue root, while queue not empty: dequeue node, visit node, if node.left exists: enqueue node.left, if node.right exists: enqueue node.right. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(w) for queue storage. This feeds into L0 by providing the traversal framework where we can add value checking. Common mistakes include not checking for null before enqueuing children (causing null pointer errors), using stack instead of queue (which gives DFS), and improper loop termination. Real code pattern: queue = [root], while queue: node = queue.pop(0), print(node.val), if node.left: queue.append(node.left), if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.trace:
1. Initialize queue = [1]
2. Dequeue 1: visit 1, enqueue 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]
3. Dequeue 2: visit 2, no children → queue = [3]
4. Dequeue 3: visit 3, no children → queue empty → done

examples.input: Tree: [A,B,C,D,E] where A=root, B=left, C=right, D=left of B, E=right of B
examples.trace:
1. Initialize queue = [A]
2. Dequeue A: visit A, enqueue B and C → queue = [B,C]
3. Dequeue B: visit B, enqueue D and E → queue = [C,D,E]
4. Dequeue C: visit C, no children → queue = [D,E]
5. Dequeue D: visit D, no children → queue = [E]
6. Dequeue E: visit E, no children → queue empty → done

ladders.title: Core Logic — Level-order Node Processing with Queue
ladders.desc: Understand how queue FIFO property enables level-by-level traversal
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight that a queue's First-In-First-Out (FIFO) property naturally produces level-order traversal. The intuition is: when we enqueue a node's children immediately after processing the node, and we always dequeue the oldest node in the queue, we guarantee that all nodes at level N are processed before any nodes at level N+1. This happens because nodes are discovered in level order, and the queue preserves that discovery order. The algorithm concept: 1) Start with root in queue (level 0), 2) Process front node (dequeue), 3) Add its children to back of queue (they become level 1), 4) Repeat. This creates a wavefront that moves down the tree level by level. Time complexity for visiting n nodes is O(n). Space complexity is O(max nodes at any level). This connects to L1 as the theoretical foundation for the queue management implementation. Common mistakes include misunderstanding FIFO vs LIFO (using stack gives DFS), not recognizing that this works for any tree shape, and confusion about why this gives level order. Real code pattern: mentally visualize queue as a line where new arrivals go to the back and we always serve from the front.

examples.input: Tree with root R, children A and B, grandchildren C and D under A
examples.output: Processing order: R, A, B, C, D
examples.trace:
1. Queue = [R] (level 0)
2. Dequeue R: process R, enqueue A and B → queue = [A,B] (level 1 ready)
3. Dequeue A: process A, enqueue C and D → queue = [B,C,D] (level 1 continues, level 2 starts)
4. Dequeue B: process B, no children → queue = [C,D] (level 1 done)
5. Dequeue C: process C, no children → queue = [D]
6. Dequeue D: process D, no children → queue empty

1. Queue = [1] (level 0)
2. Dequeue 1: process 1, enqueue 2,3 → queue = [2,3] (level 1)
3. Dequeue 2: process 2, enqueue 4,5 → queue = [3,4,5] (level 1 continues)
4. Dequeue 3: process 3, enqueue 6,7 → queue = [4,5,6,7] (level 1 done, level 2 ready)
5. Dequeue 4: process 4, no children → queue = [5,6,7]
6. Dequeue 5: process 5, no children → queue = [6,7]
7. Dequeue 6: process 6, no children → queue = [7]
8. Dequeue 7: process 7, no children → queue empty

testCases.input: Tree: root with left child only
Tree: root with right child only

testCases.expected: Processing order: root, left, right, left-left, left-right, right-left, right-right
Processing order: nodes in chain order

testCases.input: Tree: root with two children each with one child
testCases.expected: Processing order: root, left, right, left-child, right-child

testCases.input: Tree: balanced tree height 2 missing some leaves

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Tree Node Structure and Child Access
ladders.desc: Work with binary tree nodes, accessing left and right children
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the binary tree node structure and how to navigate it. A binary tree node typically has three components: a value (data), a reference to left child, and a reference to right child. The intuition is that trees are recursive structures where each node can be seen as the root of its own subtree. To traverse a tree, we need to be able to access a node's children to continue exploration. The algorithm concept: given a node object 'node', we can check if it has left child with 'node.left != null', and similarly for right child with 'node.right != null'. If children exist, we can access them directly. This is fundamental because BFS needs to enqueue children of processed nodes. Time complexity for accessing children is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the references. This connects to L2 as we need child access to implement the level-order processing. Common mistakes include null pointer errors when accessing children without null checks, misunderstanding that leaf nodes have null children, and confusion between node value and node reference. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Then node.left gives left child or None.

examples.input: Node with value 10, left child with value 5, right child with value 15
examples.trace:
1. Create node10 = Node(10)
2. Create node5 = Node(5), set node10.left = node5
3. Create node15 = Node(15), set node10.right = node15
4. Access: node10.val = 10
5. Check left: node10.left is not null, node10.left.val = 5
6. Check right: node10.right is not null, node10.right.val = 15

examples.input: Leaf node with value 7, no children
examples.trace:
1. Create node7 = Node(7)
2. By default, node7.left = None, node7.right = None
3. Access: node7.val = 7
4. Check left: node7.left is null
5. Check right: node7.right is null

testCases.input: Node value: 1, left: null, right: null
Node value: 2, left: node3, right: null
Node value: 4, left: null, right: node5
Node value: 6, left: node7, right: node8

testCases.expected: Can access children then their children

testCases.input: Node with only left child chain
Node with only right child chain

testCases.expected: Both left and right not null

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Enqueue and Dequeue Operations
ladders.desc: Perform basic queue operations: add to back (enqueue) and remove from front (dequeue)
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental queue operations needed for BFS. A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure where elements are added to the back (enqueue) and removed from the front (dequeue). The intuition is that it behaves like a real-world line: newcomers join at the end, and people are served from the front. For BFS, we enqueue nodes when we discover them (so they wait their turn) and dequeue nodes to process them in the order they were discovered. The algorithm: initialize empty queue, enqueue(item): add item to back of queue, dequeue(): remove and return item from front of queue, isEmpty(): check if queue has no items. Time complexity for both enqueue and dequeue is typically O(1) for efficient implementations. Space complexity is O(n) where n is number of items stored. This connects to L3 as we need queues to manage the tree nodes during traversal. Common mistakes include using list operations incorrectly (e.g., pop() instead of pop(0) in Python), confusing queue with stack (LIFO), and not checking isEmpty before dequeue. Real code pattern: using Python list as queue: queue = [], enqueue: queue.append(item), dequeue: queue.pop(0), isEmpty: len(queue)==0.

examples.trace:
1. Start: queue = []
2. Enqueue 1: queue = [1]
3. Enqueue 2: queue = [1,2]
4. Enqueue 3: queue = [1,2,3]
5. Dequeue: remove front 1 → returns 1, queue = [2,3]
6. Dequeue: remove front 2 → returns 2, queue = [3]
7. Dequeue: remove front 3 → returns 3, queue = []

examples.input: Mixed enqueue/dequeue: enqueue A, enqueue B, dequeue, enqueue C, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace:
1. Start: queue = []
2. Enqueue A: queue = [A]
3. Enqueue B: queue = [A,B]
4. Dequeue: remove A → returns A, queue = [B]
5. Enqueue C: queue = [B,C]
6. Dequeue: remove B → returns B, queue = [C]
7. Dequeue: remove C → returns C, queue = []

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Tree as Hierarchical Structure
ladders.desc: Recognize trees as nodes connected by parent-child relationships
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces the basic concept of a tree data structure. A tree is a hierarchical collection of nodes where each node has a value and references to child nodes. The intuition is that it resembles an upside-down real tree or organizational chart: there's a single root node at the top, nodes can have children (subordinates), and nodes without children are called leaves. Key properties: 1) One root node with no parent, 2) Each non-root node has exactly one parent, 3) No cycles (can't go from a node back to itself by following child links). In a binary tree, each node has at most two children: left and right. This is fundamental because BFS operates on this tree structure. The algorithm concept is simply understanding that trees are accessed starting from root, then moving to children, then grandchildren, etc. Time/space analysis isn't applicable here as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 as we need trees to apply queue operations to. Common mistakes include confusing trees with graphs (trees are acyclic), thinking trees must be balanced, or misunderstanding parent-child direction. Real code pattern: just the mental model of nodes with arrows to children.

examples.input: Simple tree: root A with children B and C
examples.output: Hierarchy: A (root) → children B and C
examples.trace:
1. Node A is root (no parent)
2. Node B is child of A (parent is A)
3. Node C is child of A (parent is A)
4. B and C are siblings
5. A is parent of B and C
6. B and C are leaves if they have no children

examples.input: Family analogy: Grandpa (root), Dad and Uncle (children), You and Cousin (grandchildren)
examples.output: Tree: Grandpa → Dad and Uncle, Dad → You, Uncle → Cousin
examples.trace:
1. Grandpa is root
2. Dad and Uncle are children of Grandpa
3. You are child of Dad
4. Cousin is child of Uncle
5. You and Cousin are leaves if no children
6. Hierarchy: Grandpa → Dad → You (path)

testCases.expected: Three nodes, root parent of both
Root with two children, each with two children
Not all nodes have two children

approaches.name: BST Optimization O(h)
approaches.summary: If the binary tree is a binary search tree (BST), leverage its sorted property by recursively or iteratively comparing the target with node values to eliminate half the tree at each step. This is only applicable for BSTs.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(h) where h is the tree height
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for iterative, O(h) for recursive

ladders.title: Full Problem — BST Search Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete solution to search for a value in a binary search tree using BST optimization.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution that leverages the BST property to achieve O(h) time complexity. The intuition is that in a BST, every node's left subtree contains values less than the node, and the right subtree contains values greater than the node. This sorted property allows us to eliminate half the search space at each step, similar to binary search in arrays. We can implement this recursively or iteratively. The recursive approach is elegant but uses O(h) space for the call stack, while the iterative approach uses O(1) space. The algorithm starts at the root and compares the target value with the current node's value. If they match, return true. If the target is smaller, search the left subtree; if larger, search the right subtree. If we reach a null node, the value doesn't exist in the tree. This approach only works for BSTs, not general binary trees. Common mistakes include not checking for null nodes, incorrectly handling the comparison logic, or applying this to non-BST trees. The connection to L1 is that this uses the recursive or iterative traversal as the key subroutine. Real code pattern: def searchBST(root, target): if not root: return False; if target == root.val: return True; elif target < root.val: return searchBST(root.left, target); else: return searchBST(root.right, target). Time: O(h) where h is tree height (O(log n) for balanced, O(n) for skewed). Space: O(h) recursive, O(1) iterative.

examples.trace: 1. Start at root 5, target 4 < 5 → go left to node 3. 2. At node 3, target 4 > 3 → go right to node 4. 3. At node 4, target 4 == 4 → match found, return true.
1. Start at root 10, target 8 < 10 → go left to node 5. 2. At node 5, target 8 > 5 → go right to node 7. 3. At node 7, target 8 > 7 → go right to null. 4. Null reached, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BST Recursive Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement recursive traversal that compares and navigates based on BST property.
ladders.explanation: This is the key subroutine used in the full solution: a recursive function that searches a BST. The intuition is that recursion naturally mirrors the tree structure—each call handles a subtree. The algorithm takes a node and target value. Base case: if node is null, return false (value not found). Recursive case: compare target with node value. If equal, return true. If target is less, recursively search left subtree. If greater, recursively search right subtree. This works because BST property ensures all smaller values are left, larger are right. Time complexity is O(h) as each recursive call moves one level deeper. Space complexity is O(h) for the call stack. Connection to L0: this is the core recursive implementation used in the full problem. Common mistakes: infinite recursion by not having base case, incorrect comparison direction, or modifying tree unintentionally. Real code pattern: def search(node, target): if node is None: return False; if target == node.val: return True; elif target < node.val: return search(node.left, target); else: return search(node.right, target). This subroutine is essential for the O(h) optimization.

examples.trace: 1. Call search(5,7): 7 > 5 → recursive call search(7,7). 2. Call search(7,7): 7 == 7 → return true.
1. Call search(10,3): 3 < 10 → recursive call search(5,3). 2. Call search(5,3): 3 < 5 → recursive call search(None,3). 3. Call search(None,3): node is None → return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — BST Property Navigation
ladders.desc: The central technique of comparing target with node value to decide left or right traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic that enables O(h) search: using the BST property to navigate. Intuition: in a BST, values are organized such that for any node, all values in the left subtree are smaller, and all in the right subtree are larger. This creates a sorted order that allows binary search-like elimination. The technique is simple: at each node, compare the target value with the node's value. If target equals node value, we found it. If target is less, we know the target can only be in the left subtree (if it exists). If target is greater, it can only be in the right subtree. This decision point is repeated recursively or iteratively until we find the target or reach a null pointer. This reduces the search space by half at each step in a balanced tree, giving O(log n) time, but in worst-case skewed trees, it's O(n). The space depends on implementation. Connection to L1: this comparison and navigation logic is what the recursive function implements. Common mistakes: not strictly following the property (e.g., going left when target is greater), or assuming the tree is balanced. Real code pattern: if target < node.val: go left; elif target > node.val: go right; else: found. This logic is the heart of BST optimization.

examples.trace: Compare 7 < 10 → true, so navigation decision is to go to left subtree.
Compare 6 > 4 → true, so navigation decision is to go to right subtree.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Node Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing values at tree nodes to make traversal decisions.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves comparing the target value with a node's value in a tree structure. Intuition: in any tree search, we need to check if the current node holds the target value. For BST optimization, this comparison also dictates the next step. The operation is straightforward: evaluate whether target is less than, equal to, or greater than the node's value. This requires understanding of basic comparison operators and how to access node attributes (like node.val). In programming, this is typically an if-elif-else block. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: this comparison is the action taken at each node during navigation. Common mistakes: using wrong comparison operators, comparing with wrong node attribute, or not handling equality case. Real code pattern: if target == node.val: # found; elif target < node.val: # go left; else: # go right. This building block is fundamental for implementing the core logic.

examples.trace: Compare 8 == 8 → true, so result is equal.
Compare 5 > 3 → true, so target is greater than node value.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Value
ladders.desc: Retrieving the value stored in a tree node.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation: accessing the data (value) stored in a tree node. Intuition: in tree data structures, each node typically contains a value and references to child nodes. To perform any operation like search, we first need to read this value. In most implementations, nodes are objects or structs with attributes such as 'val' for the value, 'left' for the left child, and 'right' for the right child. Accessing node.val gives the integer or other data type stored. This is a basic O(1) time operation. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: this value is what we compare with the target in the comparison step. Common mistakes: trying to access value from a null node (causing errors), or misunderstanding the node structure. Real code pattern: value = node.val. This operation is prerequisite for any tree algorithm.

examples.trace: Access node.val → returns 5.
Access node.val → returns 12.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree Definition
ladders.desc: Understanding what a BST is: a binary tree with sorted property.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree where for every node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are greater. Intuition: this property imposes an order that makes searching efficient. It's like having a sorted array but in tree form. Key points: it's a binary tree (each node has at most two children), and the sorted property must hold for all nodes. This definition is why we can use comparison to navigate—it guarantees that if a value exists, it will be in the correct subtree based on comparison. Time and space aren't applicable here as it's a definition. Connection to L4: knowing a node's value is part of understanding BST structure. Common mistakes: confusing BST with general binary tree, or not realizing the property must be recursive. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. This foundation is essential for the entire approach.

examples.trace: Check root 5: left subtree has 3 (3<5), right has 7 (7>5). Property holds.
Check root 5: left subtree has 7 (7>5 violates property), so not a BST.
